content.problemStatement:
As usual, Tom and Jerry are fighting. Tom has strength TSTSTS and Jerry has strength JSJSJS. You are given TSTSTS and your task is to find the number of possible values of JSJSJS such that Jerry wins the following game.

The game consists of one or more turns. In each turn:


If both TSTSTS and JSJSJS are even, their values get divided by 222 and the game proceeds with the next turn.
If both TSTSTS and JSJSJS are odd, a tie is declared and the game ends.
If TSTSTS is even and JSJSJS is odd, Tom wins the game.
If TSTSTS is odd and JSJSJS is even, Jerry wins the game.
Find the number of possible initial values of JSJSJS such that 1≤JS≤TS1 \le JS \le TS1≤JS≤TS, there is no tie and Jerry wins the game.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer TSTSTS.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the number of values of JSJSJS such that Jerry wins the game.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We must have JS=TS=1JS = TS = 1JS=TS=1, but in this case, the game ends and a tie is declared in the first turn.
Example case 2: If JS∈{2,4,6,8,10}JS \in \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}JS∈{2,4,6,8,10}, then JSJSJS is even and TSTSTS is odd, so Jerry wins the game in the first turn. Any odd value of JSJSJS leads to a tie instead.
